narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the three biggest influences pushing toward this higher failure risk were your Year 3 grade decile, your LSAT score, and—countering that somewhat—your upper-middle family income bracket. Your Year 3 decile of 2 had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it placed you well below the typical 6th decile among students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustaining strong performance. Similarly, your LSAT score of 25 stood out as a major concern, far under the average 37 seen in passers, which the model weighs heavily as a key indicator of readiness for legal studies and exam success. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income helped nudge the prediction toward passing, aligning with the majority of bar passers who came from similar backgrounds, offering some stability the model associates with better outcomes. Compared to typical admitted students—who often have undergraduate GPAs around 3.24, much higher than your 2.50, and Year 1 deciles near 6—your profile showed gaps in these core academic metrics that tipped the scales. Your choice to attend full-time also matches the norm for passers, but it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort; it's the model highlighting where your strengths and challenges align with historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and focus on building a stronger academic record, perhaps through targeted coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile rankings. Gathering letters of recommendation that speak to your resilience and growth could also help reframe your story in a future application. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor and strengthen your path to law school.
